tumors.name: Meningioma
tumors.subtitle: Usually starts in the lining around the brain or spinal cord
tumors.summary: Meningiomas are among the most common primary brain tumors. Many are slow-growing, though higher-grade meningiomas can behave more aggressively.
tumors.symptoms: Headaches, seizures, weakness, vision changes, or no symptoms if found incidentally.
tumors.diagnosis: MRI is commonly used first. If surgery is done, pathology helps confirm the exact grade.
tumors.treatment: Observation for some tumors, surgery, focused radiation, or radiation after surgery when needed.
tumors.questions: Is this tumor pressing on nearby brain tissue? What grade is it? Is monitoring safe, or is treatment recommended now?

tumors.name: Glioblastoma
tumors.subtitle: A fast-growing grade 4 astrocytic tumor
tumors.summary: Glioblastoma is an aggressive brain tumor that usually occurs in adults and often needs treatment quickly after diagnosis.
tumors.symptoms: Headaches, seizures, confusion, weakness, speech trouble, or personality changes depending on location.
tumors.diagnosis: MRI plus surgery or biopsy for pathology confirmation. Molecular testing may also help guide care.
tumors.treatment: Surgery when possible, followed by radiation, chemotherapy, and sometimes clinical trials.
tumors.questions: How much tumor was removed? What is the treatment goal now? Are clinical trials or supportive care services a fit?

tumors.name: Pituitary Tumor
tumors.subtitle: Usually grows in the hormone-controlling pituitary gland
tumors.summary: Most pituitary tumors are noncancerous and slow-growing, but they can still cause serious hormone or vision problems.
tumors.symptoms: Headaches, vision changes, fatigue, menstrual changes, low sex drive, or symptoms from too much hormone production.
tumors.diagnosis: Pituitary-focused MRI plus blood tests to check hormone levels and sometimes vision testing.
tumors.treatment: Monitoring, medication for some hormone-producing tumors, surgery, and sometimes radiation.
tumors.questions: Is the tumor making hormones? Is vision at risk? Which specialists should be involved, such as endocrinology or neurosurgery?

tumors.name: Astrocytoma
tumors.subtitle: A glioma that begins in astrocytes
tumors.summary: Astrocytoma is a broad group of tumors that can be lower grade and slower growing or higher grade and more aggressive.
tumors.symptoms: Seizures, headaches, weakness, changes in thinking, or balance problems depending on tumor location.
tumors.diagnosis: MRI followed by biopsy or surgery to identify the grade and tumor features.
tumors.treatment: Surgery, monitoring, radiation, chemotherapy, or a combination depending on grade and symptoms.
tumors.questions: What grade is this astrocytoma? Is it considered low grade or high grade? What does that mean for treatment timing?

tumors.name: Oligodendroglioma
tumors.subtitle: An IDH-mutated tumor defined by specific genetic findings
tumors.summary: Oligodendroglioma can be slow- or faster-growing. Diagnosis depends on both pathology and specific tumor genetics.
tumors.symptoms: Seizures, headaches, weakness, or changes in thinking or speech.
tumors.diagnosis: MRI plus tissue testing that looks for an IDH mutation and a 1p/19q codeletion.
tumors.treatment: Surgery first when possible, then radiation, chemotherapy, or clinical trials based on grade and remaining tumor.
tumors.questions: Were the molecular markers confirmed? What does grade 2 vs grade 3 mean in my case? What follow-up schedule is expected?

tumors.name: Vestibular Schwannoma
tumors.subtitle: Also called acoustic neuroma
tumors.summary: Vestibular schwannoma is usually a slow-growing tumor that develops from the hearing and balance nerves near the inner ear.
tumors.symptoms: One-sided hearing loss, ringing in the ear, dizziness, balance problems, or facial numbness if larger.
tumors.diagnosis: Hearing evaluation and MRI are commonly used to confirm the tumor and assess size.
tumors.treatment: Observation, stereotactic radiosurgery, or surgery depending on size, symptoms, and growth rate.
tumors.questions: Is the tumor growing? How might treatment affect hearing or facial nerve function? Is watchful waiting reasonable?

tumors.name: Medulloblastoma
tumors.subtitle: A fast-growing tumor that often begins in the cerebellum
tumors.summary: Medulloblastoma is more common in children but can also occur in adults. It tends to grow quickly and may spread through cerebrospinal fluid.
tumors.symptoms: Balance trouble, headaches, nausea, vomiting, blurred vision, sleepiness, or symptoms from pressure in the brain.
tumors.diagnosis: MRI plus surgery or biopsy for pathology, along with staging to see whether it has spread in the brain or spine.
tumors.treatment: Surgery followed by radiation and chemotherapy in many patients.
tumors.questions: Has the tumor spread? What risk group applies? How will treatment affect long-term learning, fatigue, or hormone health?
